designs.name: THE EMBEDDED FORGE — nerves drop into the work surfaces at the canon ports
designs.oneLineConcept: The brain voyages above; its two real nerve bundles drop straight down into the editor (left port x=-4.8) and the preview (right port x=+4.8), and the spinal cord plugs into the command line — the work surfaces are mounted IN the canvas as new in-scene panels at the exact world-points the unchanged nerves already terminate on, so a write literally travels the nerve and lights the tool it lands on.
designs.nerveWiring:
The connection is coordinate coincidence in screen space, refreshed every frame — exactly the canon contract, reused with zero shader/geometry change.

THREE NEW PRODUCT IN-SCENE PANELS, each a sibling of <SuperbrainHUD/> inside the ONE existing <Canvas> tree (WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:207, alongside the scene at 231-242 — a NEW product component <ForgePorts/> added there, NOT a 2nd canvas):

1. EDITOR -> LEFT PORT. A new <Html position={[-4.8,-1.7,0.0]} zIndexRange={[100,0]}> wrapping the Monaco editor (CodeCanvas), inner div transform:'translate(-50%,-100%)' — byte-for-byte the anchoring pattern at SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1046-1047. The left peripheral bundle (45 wires) terminates at exactly this world-point (NervousSystem.tsx:204 leftTargetX=-4.8, port entry (-4.8,-1.7,0)). The panel's BOTTOM-CENTER lands on the wire tip; the editor body grows UPWARD from there. Real nerves plug straight in, zero lab edit.

2. PREVIEW -> RIGHT PORT. A new <Html position={[4.8,-1.5,0.0]} zIndexRange={[100,0]}> wrapping LivePreview's iframe, same translate(-50%,-100%) (mirror of SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1097-1098). The right peripheral bundle (45 wires, NervousSystem.tsx:205 rightTargetX=4.8, entry (4.8,-1.5,0)) plugs into the preview's bottom-center.

3. COMMAND LINE -> SPINAL PORT. The spinal bundle (35 wires) terminates at (0,-2.6,1.5) — a visual rendezvous, NOT a 3D bind (it createPortals into screen-space #hud-portal-root). So the command line stays a flat-DOM bar positioned with CSS where (0,-2.6,1.5) projects (bottom-center, ~bottom:34px like superbrain.css:1212), and we REPRODUCE the .command-bar::after wire-glow gradient (superbrain.css:1239) on the new bar so light still pours down from the spinal tip into it. This is the one port that is product-CSS-only and needs no Html.

CRITICAL HONESTY ON "transform": my assigned direction says <Html transform>, but the canon consoles deliberately OMIT drei's transform prop (SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1040-1045) to skip R3F matrix scaling and re-project every frame as a flat pinned DOM panel. For Monaco + an iframe, NON-transform is strictly correct AND cheaper (transform mode wraps the surface in a per-frame CSS3D matrix — costly for Monaco's own DOM/workers and the iframe). So the faithful, GPU-sane build uses the canon NON-transform + translate(-50%,-100%) pattern, not literal <Html transform>. I am flagging this as a refinement of the assigned wording, not a violation of its spirit (surfaces still sit AT the canon ports, nerves still plug in unchanged).
designs.composition:
A vertical voyage, read top-to-bottom as one organism:

- TOP (the voyage, sacred-canon, untouched): the docked brain floats in the upper band — full SuperbrainScene + cortex + aura + the 3 canon nerve bundles, rendering byte-identical. The brain is smaller (CSS stage resize only, the SuperbrainShell.jsx:37 .sb-brain-stage approach) but STILL travelling — sway, Float bob, breath all live. Infinite space continues above and around it.
- MID-LEFT (the editor port): Monaco hangs off the left nerve at x=-4.8, a glass slab whose bottom-center is the plug. Tabs along its top.
- MID-RIGHT (the preview port): the live iframe hangs off the right nerve at x=+4.8, mirror slab, browser-chrome dots on top.
- The two side nerves arc out of the brainstem, swoop down and OUTWARD, and pour into the two slabs — the brain literally reaching its hands into the forge.
- BOTTOM (the spinal port): the command line spans the bottom-center where the 35-wire spinal bundle plugs in, the wire-glow gradient bleeding down into it. The Voyage toggle sits at its left edge.
- The kept .topbar (sovereignty row FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE + Supervised pill) stays at the very top as the unchanged ported chrome.
- BuildFeed (the agent's running narration) docks as a thin rail between the brain and the slabs, or along the spinal bar — so every dispatched step is visible AT the moment its packet fires down the nerve.
designs.controlBehaviour:
A write is a packet, and the packet is REAL — driven by the same cognitionBus + shader uniforms the canon already runs, no new mechanism invented.

CONTINUOUS BASELINE: uTime drives a flowing data-packet per wire (fract(vUv.x*4 - uTime*vSpeed + vPhase), NervousSystem.tsx:63-64,71) on all three bundles always — the brain is never idle, packets perpetually trickle down into the editor, preview, and command line even at rest. The voyage breathes (uBreath, per-wire aPulse) so the cables into the forge pulse with the cortex.

A WRITE LANDS (the payoff): when the agent edits a file, aiosAdapter.ts already publishes {type:'agent-dispatch', detail:'tool engaged: <name>'} (verified aiosAdapter.ts:111-114). SuperbrainScene.tsx:1066 catches that and shoves burst.current.intensity>=0.45; a 'directive' shoves >=0.6 (1052); SKILL MASTERED =1 (1088). uBurst (module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM, NervousSystem.tsx:86,192-194) multiplies wire brightness by (1+uBurst) (line 73) — so on a write, ALL the nerves SURGE BRIGHT and the packet streaks visibly down into the slab the work landed on. The editor port lights as the file is written; the preview port lights as it re-renders.

PER-PORT TARGETING (the one product-side enrichment): so the EDITOR nerve specifically brightens on a code write and the PREVIEW nerve on a render, the product write-path publishes a port-tagged dispatch and <ForgePorts/> reads the same bus to flash that ONE slab's frame (a CSS box-shadow/border pulse on the lit panel). The 3D nerve still surges globally (canon), but the slab edge-glow makes it READ as 'this nerve, this tool'. Pure product — subscribeCognition is exported lib.

A HOLD (approval): on {type:'approval-required'}, uHold eases 0->1 (shared SCENE_UNIFORMS.uHold, SuperbrainScene.tsx:1024-1038); wires dim to 0.3 (mix(1.0,0.3,uHold), NervousSystem.tsx:75), cortex amber dims, breath freezes — the WHOLE organism goes quiet, the forge held mid-strike, until the operator resolves. The kept .approval-panel surfaces the diff. One signal, whole-organism — the mind visibly deferring to him.
designs.themeFit: This is the most literal possible embodiment of "the mind controlling its whole screen through its nervous system." The brain does not sit NEXT TO its tools — it is WIRED INTO them. The forge is not a separate app docked below; it is the brain's hands, hanging off its own nerves in the same infinite space the brain voyages through. The voyage never stops: the docked brain still sways, bobs, breathes, and travels into the deep-vast knowledge-space above, while its peripheral branches reach DOWN into the editor and preview and its spinal cord drops into the command line. When the agent writes a file, you SEE the thought leave the cortex, race down the cable as a packet, and land glowing on the surface it changed — cognition becoming creation along a visible nerve. When it asks his approval, the whole nervous system goes still and waits. The forge is the superbrain's body made productive, never a frozen panel — exactly the autonomous AI-OS superbrain manufacturing reality through its own anatomy.

designs.feasibility:
PRODUCT-SIDE, NO LAB EDIT, NO npm run port (the whole core of this design):
- The new <ForgePorts/> component with two <Html position={[-4.8,-1.7,0]}> / [4.8,-1.5,0] wrappers around CodeCanvas + LivePreview, reusing the canon translate(-50%,-100%) anchor. NEW product file — never a ported file, so it is freely editable and survives port. Mounted as a sibling in WorkspaceCanvas's existing tree... BUT WorkspaceCanvas.tsx IS a ported file (frontend/src/superbrain/), so I CANNOT add the sibling there product-side. THE PRODUCT-SAFE MOUNT: render <ForgePorts/> from SuperbrainShell.jsx is impossible (it's outside the Canvas). HONEST RESOLUTION — to put a new <Html> INSIDE the one canvas without editing ported WorkspaceCanvas.tsx, the cleanest path is a small lab seam: add a product-children slot to WorkspaceCanvas in the lab (one prop, render {children} inside <Canvas>) + npm run port ONCE; thereafter all forge panels are product children. That seam is a ~3-line lab edit that touches NO geometry/shader and needs NO re-golden of the nerves (it adds nothing visible by itself). This is the ONE modest lab touch and I am naming it honestly rather than pretending it's pure product.
- Un-hiding/relocating the ports, the command-line bar at the spinal projection, the wire-glow ::after reproduction, the slab edge-glow on a port-tagged write, subscribing to cognitionBus, the CSS stage-resize dock — ALL product (manufacturing.css / shell.css / new product CSS). drei + @monaco-editor/react are already product deps.

GENUINELY NEEDS LAB + npm run port: NOTHING about the nerves themselves — leftTargetX/rightTargetX/spinal points and the shader are UNCHANGED (that is the entire virtue of this direction). The only lab edit is the optional children-slot seam on WorkspaceCanvas above; if that is refused, the fallback is option (c) faux-nerve overlay to flat-DOM slabs (lower fidelity, fully product).

GPU REALITY (16GB laptop + local inference): one Monaco + one iframe as NON-transform <Html> composite as normal DOM layers over WebGL — cheap, NOT re-matrix'd per frame (the reason to refuse literal transform). Real costs to respect: PostFX fullscreen pass + cortex Voronoi (single heaviest shader, SuperbrainScene.tsx:732) already run; the 115-tube bundle is already drawn. Mitigations: keep both slabs in STATIC-positioned inner wrappers (only the <Html> wrapper re-projects, not Monaco's layout); debounce LivePreview's key={srcDoc} remount (LivePreview.jsx:117) so keystrokes don't thrash; park/freeze the preview iframe when offscreen; honor TIER_DPR. Net: well within budget for one editor + one preview.
designs.functionalWiring:
The agent's REAL actions already flow on the bus — I wire the forge to consume them, no new event system.

WRITE PATH IN (agent -> editor): aiosAdapter.ts already publishes per-tool dispatches (verified publishCognition at aiosAdapter.ts:111-114, detail:`tool engaged: ${tool}`). For the forge to (a) surge the nerve and (b) actually update the editor: when the agent's turn produces a create_file/edit_file, the product write-path pushes the file content into the same files state <ForgePorts/> owns (lift Workbench.jsx's useState files/active into <ForgePorts/> or a small product store), AND publishes the dispatch. Result: the editor tab updates with the agent's code while the left nerve surges bright — you watch the brain type into its own editor down the nerve. The active tab auto-switches to the file the agent touched, so the lit slab shows the write landing.

RENDER PATH (editor -> preview): LivePreview already recomputes srcDoc from files (LivePreview.jsx), so the agent's write flows editor->preview automatically; the right nerve surges on the render dispatch. Debounced so the voyage doesn't stutter.

DIRECTIVE PATH (command line -> brain): CommandLine.jsx already reuses sendDirective; SuperbrainHUD's handleSubmit publishes {type:'directive'} before onDirective (SuperbrainHUD.tsx:808). So typing into the spinal command line shoves burst>=0.6 (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1052) — the spinal nerve flares as the directive enters the mind. Same wiring, new bar position.

APPROVAL PATH (hold): a human_required pause publishes {type:'approval-required'} -> uHold dims every nerve to 0.3, freezes breath; the kept .approval-panel shows the diff; resolve republishes and the forge resumes. The held write sits visibly un-applied in the editor until he approves — the diff IS the pending edit. RED-zone stays hard-blocked per his policy regardless of approval.

READ-BACK (for a backend file read, if the agent reads disk): publish a 'knowledge-acquired' on the bus (already a type, cognitionBus.ts) so the intake reads it and a packet runs the nerve inward — symmetric to writes.
designs.risks:
1. THE ONE LAB SEAM I cannot avoid: putting a new <Html> inside the single canvas requires either editing ported WorkspaceCanvas.tsx (forbidden product-side) OR adding a product-children slot in the lab + one port. I chose the slot (geometry-neutral, no re-golden). If he refuses ANY lab touch, this direction degrades to a faux-nerve overlay (option c) — lower fidelity, must be his-browser-validated. I will not pretend the embedded path is 100% zero-lab.

2. CAMERA/FRAMING DRIFT (the exact bug that killed the shell): the nerve endpoints are frozen to a specific camera (Z~8.5, 45deg FOV — WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:209, NervousSystem.tsx:171-174 tabX frozen on purpose). Docking the brain into a SHORTER band changes effective framing, so the nerve TIPS project to different screen pixels and the slabs separate from the plugs. MITIGATION: do NOT shrink via a clipped band — keep the SAME camera/aspect and dock by scaling the whole stage uniformly (CSS transform:scale on .sb-brain-stage) so projection ratios are preserved, OR accept that the slab anchors must be tuned to the docked framing in HIS browser with before/after screenshots. This is the make-or-break detail; the shell failed precisely here.

3. NON-transform vs transform: I am overriding the assigned wording (transform) to non-transform for fidelity+GPU. If he specifically wants the literal CSS3D transform look, that is a different, costlier build — flag for his call.

4. GPU headroom: Monaco + iframe + 115 tubes + Voronoi + PostFX on a 16GB inference box. Measured mitigations listed (static inner wrappers, debounced preview remount, park-when-offscreen, TIER_DPR). Must verify frame budget in his browser, not assume.

5. FIDELITY laws: the brain scene + nerve geometry/shaders render UNCHANGED (this design changes none), but the docked composition is a VISUAL change — needs canon tag + goldens + his-browser before/after parity before shipping, per the sacred UI laws.

6. The spinal command-bar plug is a hand-tuned coincidence (FOV/viewport); re-homing the bar must reproduce .command-bar::after or the brain-to-bar connection reads dead (documented pitfall).

designs.name: THE FORGE OF FOUR NERVES — a re-aimed nervous system that plugs into the work surfaces
designs.oneLineConcept: A canonical lab re-route of the nervous system: the spinal cord splits into FOUR named nerves — Editor, Preview, Command, and a new Tool-Rack bundle — that physically plug into in-scene work panels embedded at the new port coordinates, so a real write travels the nerve as a data packet and lights the editor it lands on, the brain commanding its forge through its own wiring while it voyages.
designs.nerveWiring:
The redesign re-aims the existing three bundles and adds a fourth, ALL edited in the lab file `GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/components/canvas/NervousSystem.tsx` then `npm run port`'d to overwrite the byte-identical `frontend/src/superbrain/components/canvas/NervousSystem.tsx`. The shared spinal scaffold (deepCore (0,0.5,-0.4) -> stemExit (0,-0.5,-0.5) -> spinalDrop (0,-1.2,-0.4)) and the SpiralWireCurve/addWireBundle helper and the WIRE_FRAGMENT shader are kept BYTE-FOR-BYTE — only the per-bundle terminal control points (and the leftTargetX/rightTargetX literals at lines 204-205) change. The new port literals:

- EDITOR NERVE (re-home of the LEFT bundle, 45 wires): change leftTargetX from -4.8 to -3.6 and the two terminal points to swoop into the editor panel's TOP-LEFT shoulder: ...spinalDrop -> (-2.0,-1.9,-0.2) -> (-3.6,-2.1,-0.05) -> PORT ENTRY (-3.6,-1.55,0.05). This is the FAT bundle (the editor is where most writes land).
- PREVIEW NERVE (re-home of the RIGHT bundle, 45 wires): change rightTargetX from 4.8 to 3.6 and terminate at the preview panel's top-right shoulder: ...spinalDrop -> (2.0,-1.9,-0.2) -> (3.6,-2.1,-0.05) -> PORT ENTRY (3.6,-1.45,0.05).
- COMMAND NERVE (the spinal bundle, 35 wires, ESSENTIALLY UNCHANGED): keep the canon plug (0,-2.0,0.2) -> (0,-2.6,1.5) — it already aims at the bottom-center command line and never depended on 3D projection. Left as-is to minimise canon churn; the command line panel sits where this projects.
- TOOL-RACK NERVE (NEW addWireBundle call, 24 wires): the genuinely new port. A thinner bundle splitting LATER down the cord so it reads as a distinct branch: spinalDrop -> (0,-1.7,-0.2) -> (0,-2.3,0.9) -> PORT ENTRY (0,-2.95,1.55), terminating just under the command line, fanning into a horizontal rack of tool-chips. Uses the same allColors palette and baseSpread 0.06.

The matching <Html> port PANELS move with the endpoints. Because SuperbrainHUD.tsx is ALSO ported, I do NOT edit it; instead I REMOVE the two console <Html> blocks from the lab SuperbrainHUD (or leave them and hide via product CSS) and mount the NEW work panels from a NEW PRODUCT component, `ForgePorts.jsx`, rendered as a sibling of <SuperbrainHUD/> INSIDE the one existing <Canvas> tree. Each panel is a non-transform <Html position={[x,y,z]} zIndexRange={[100,0]}> with inner `transform: translate(-50%,-100%)`, copying the canon anchor pattern EXACTLY: editor at [-3.6,-1.55,0.05], preview at [3.6,-1.45,0.05], command at [0,-2.6,1.5], tool-rack at [0,-2.95,1.55]. The nerve tips and the panel anchors share the same world point, so the re-routed bundles plug straight in — a real geometric bind, not a faux overlay.
designs.composition:
The brain does NOT shrink to a band. It stays the LEAD, hovering upper-center in its infinite voyaging space (canon scene + Float bob + sway untouched), and the work surfaces are its FORGE hung from its own nerves in the lower hemisphere — the whole screen is one organism, not a brain-on-top / editor-below split.

Vertical zones (single fullscreen canvas):
- TOP ~46vh: the canon brain, cortex, aura, sky=VOYAGE, surface=WEB — pixel-identical to home. The topbar sovereignty row (FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE) stays in #hud-portal-root.
- MID ~6vh: the spinal/branch region where the four nerves visibly split out of the brain stem and fan to their ports — this is the 'control bus' made visible.
- LOWER ~48vh: the FORGE. Editor port lower-left (the fat Editor nerve plugging into its top-left shoulder), Preview port lower-right (Preview nerve into its top-right shoulder), Command line bottom-center (the canon spinal plug), and the Tool-Rack a thin horizontal strip of live tool-chips directly under the command line (the new nerve).

The panels are arranged so each nerve enters from ABOVE at the panel's shoulder and the panel hangs BELOW the port (translate -100% Y), exactly like the canon consoles hang below their ports — so the brain reads as literally SUSPENDING its tools on its wiring. There is no 'dock band'; the forge floats in the same cosmos the brain travels through (a cheap product CSS cosmos/seam fills the deep background behind the canvas, continuing the infinite).
designs.controlBehaviour:
Reading the brain CONTROL a tool, channel by channel — all three shader channels are UNCHANGED, so behaviour is canon-correct by construction:

1. PACKET (uTime, unchanged): every nerve continuously carries flowing data packets — fract(vUv.x*4.0 - uTime*vSpeed + vPhase) — at rest. The forge is always faintly alive, the bus idling. Each of the four bundles has its own per-wire aSpeed/aPhase so the Editor nerve and Preview nerve pulse independently, reading as four distinct conduits.

2. BURST (uBurst, module WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM, unchanged driver): when a REAL action fires, the scene root already sets burstRef.intensity (directive>=0.6, agent-dispatch 'tool engaged'>=0.45, SKILL MASTERED=1) and the wires surge brightness *(1+uBurst). Because ALL four bundles share the one merged geometry and the one WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM, a write surges the WHOLE bus — then the LANDED port also lights via its panel (see functionalWiring): a code-emit pulses the Editor panel's port-glow, a preview refresh pulses the Preview panel. So the eye sees: brain thinks -> whole nervous system brightens -> the packet arrives -> the specific tool port flares. That IS the brain commanding a tool down a nerve.

3. HOLD (uHold, shared SCENE_UNIFORMS leaf, unchanged): on approval-required the entire bus dims to 0.3 and the cortex amber and breath freeze — the forge goes quiet, hands off the tools, defers to the operator. On approve/resolve it surges back. The four-nerve forge inherits this for free: when the mind holds for approval, the editor/preview/tool-rack nerves ALL go quiet together — a supervised mind visibly staying its own hands.

The TOOL-RACK nerve makes 'which tool' legible: each agent-dispatch 'tool engaged: <name>' lights the matching chip in the rack (read/write/edit/search/verify) as the burst rolls down its branch — you watch the turn think tool-by-tool, the new nerve carrying the dispatch to the rack.
designs.themeFit: The core theme is an autonomous AI-OS superbrain travelling constant into the deep-vast infinite, never frozen. This form keeps the brain VOYAGING (canon scene, Float bob, sway, VOYAGE sky — all untouched and still fullscreen-scale, the lead) while revealing that its nervous system is how it REACHES OUT and works the infinite — the four nerves are the mind extending its will into matter (code, preview, command, tools) without ever stopping its travel. The forge is not a separate app pane bolted below; it is grown FROM the brain's own spinal cord, suspended on its wiring, floating in the same cosmos the brain sails. A write is the mind reaching down a nerve and shaping the world at the far end. The HOLD channel renders the thesis — a sovereign mind that voyages autonomously yet visibly defers to its operator, going quiet across its whole body when approval is owed. Nothing freezes: even at rest the packets flow and the brain drifts onward. The new fourth nerve embodies growth — the bus can sprout new conduits as the mind gains tools, which is exactly an OS superbrain expanding into more of the infinite.

designs.feasibility:
HONEST PRODUCT vs LAB split for THIS design:

LAB + `npm run port` (unavoidable for this direction — it IS the direction):
- `GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/components/canvas/NervousSystem.tsx`: change leftTargetX -3.6, rightTargetX 3.6, the four editor/preview terminal control points, and ADD one addWireBundle(24,...) call for the tool-rack nerve. ~15 lines. Shader, SpiralWireCurve, addWireBundle, palette, spinal scaffold all untouched.
- The lab `SuperbrainHUD.tsx`: REMOVE (or leave-and-product-hide) the two console <Html> blocks at [-4.8,-1.7] / [4.8,-1.5] since their ports moved; the work panels replace them. If removed, this is a second ported-file lab edit.
- This is a CANON CHANGE -> triggers the FIDELITY laws: canon re-tag + re-golden, before/after screenshots, parity proven in HIS browser. The brain scene itself is pixel-identical; the diff is only nerve routing + new panels, so goldens should diff cleanly in the brain region.

PRODUCT-SIDE (no lab, no port):
- NEW `ForgePorts.jsx` mounting the four work panels as non-transform <Html> at the new port world-points (editor, preview, command, tool-rack), each wrapping Monaco / iframe / command line / chip-rack. New file, not ported.
- Subscribing each panel to cognitionBus for its port-glow (product lib, importable).
- Product CSS for the panels, the cosmos/seam background, port-glow keyframes.
- The tool-rack chip lighting off existing agent-dispatch events.

GPU REALITY (16GB laptop + local inference): the real cost. Monaco + a live iframe inside non-transform <Html> composite as normal DOM layers over WebGL (cheap projection-pin, NOT drei `transform` which re-projects a 3D plane every frame — I deliberately use non-transform like the canon consoles). The 115->139-tube bundle (adding 24 wires) is a minor merged-geometry bump, still ONE draw call. Mitigations: keep Monaco in a static-positioned inner wrapper so only the <Html> anchor re-projects (a 2D point), not the editor; debounce the iframe srcDoc remount (LivePreview remounts on key={srcDoc}) and PARK the iframe when the preview port is occluded/offscreen; respect TIER_DPR; the cortex Voronoi remains the heaviest pass so leave headroom by NOT adding postFX. Net: feasible on the target machine, with the iframe as the watch item.
designs.functionalWiring:
The agent's REAL actions already flow on cognitionBus via aiosAdapter.ts — verified: tool_call -> publishCognition({type:'agent-dispatch', detail:'tool engaged: <tool>'}) (line 111), CODE EMITTED on code frames (line 257), AUTONOMOUS ACTION on autonomous writes (line 289), VERIFICATION GREEN/RED (line 132), approval-required (line 244) / approval-resolved (line 348). So the forge needs ZERO new event plumbing on the scene side — the re-routed nerves already surge on every real turn because SuperbrainScene's subscriber maps these to burstRef/holdRef (unchanged).

What ForgePorts wires:
1. EDITOR PORT: Monaco's value is fed by the SAME write path. On a CODE EMITTED / write_file / edit_file event, the editor (a) loads the file content the agent wrote and (b) flashes a `.port-glow` at its top-left shoulder (where the Editor nerve plugs) — the packet 'arrives'. The editor subscribes to agent-dispatch where detail matches write/edit and to the 'CODE EMITTED' knowledge-acquired event; it reads the emitted code from event.data (aiosAdapter already carries code/language on the code frame) or via the product read endpoint for the touched path.
2. PREVIEW PORT: the iframe srcDoc rebuilds from the current editor doc (debounced); a 'SYNTHESIS COMPLETE' / preview-refresh flashes the Preview nerve's shoulder glow.
3. COMMAND PORT: the existing CommandLine reuses sendDirective, which publishes {type:'directive'} (already surges the spinal nerve via burst>=0.6 and pushes the camera) before running the turn.
4. TOOL-RACK PORT: subscribes to agent-dispatch 'tool engaged: <name>', lighting the matching chip (read/write/edit/grep/verify) for the duration of that tool's burst. AUTONOMOUS ACTION lights the chip gold (the earned-autonomy path). This makes the new fourth nerve carry legible, real per-tool dispatch.

The payoff: because burst/hold/packet are driven by the real adapter and the nerves are now geometrically aimed at the panels, the FORGE genuinely DOES the work — type a directive, the command nerve surges, the turn's tool_calls roll bursts down the bus and light the tool-rack chips, a write lands and the editor port flares with the new code, the preview refreshes, and if the mind owes an approval the whole forge dims and waits.
designs.risks:
1. CANON CHURN (biggest): editing NervousSystem.tsx is a canon change under FIDELITY laws — re-tag, re-golden, his-browser parity, before/after screenshots REQUIRED before it ships. The brain itself is pixel-identical so the goldens that cover the cortex/aura should pass; only the nerve-region + lower-panel goldens change. If he rejects the new routing, revert is clean (it's localized to control points).
2. THE FROZEN-CAMERA COUPLING: tabX=4.82 and all endpoints are tuned to camera Z~8.5, 45deg FOV, 16:9. The new ports (-3.6/+3.6, the rack at y=-2.95) must be re-validated to project to the intended on-screen panel anchors at the SHIPPING viewport; a different aspect ratio shifts the plug. The canon consoles had the same constraint — I inherit it, must verify in his browser at his resolution.
3. GPU: the live iframe is the watch item (remount churn on keystroke + compositing over WebGL). If it stutters the voyage, park/debounce it; worst case make preview a manual 'refresh' tap instead of live.
4. PORTED-FILE PAIRING: the nerve endpoints (NervousSystem.tsx) and any console removal (SuperbrainHUD.tsx) are BOTH ported — they must move together in the same lab commit + one `npm run port`, or the old consoles render at dead -4.8/+4.8 ports while the nerves aim at -3.6/+3.6.
5. SINGLE-CANVAS LAW: ForgePorts MUST mount inside the existing <Canvas> tree as a sibling of SuperbrainHUD — never a 2nd canvas (re-runs boot, doubles GPU, cross-wires the module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM/SCENE_UNIFORMS).
6. NERVE-OUTSIDE-FLOAT RIGIDITY: NervousSystem is mounted outside <Float> on purpose so bottom wires stay rigid to static UI. The new panels are statically positioned (projection-pinned, they don't bob with the brain), so the rigid nerve tips and static panel anchors stay aligned — consistent with canon; do NOT put the panels inside Float or the plug separates.
7. Tool-rack as a NEW bundle adds 24 tubes to the merged geometry — verify the seeded braid still looks clean and the extra draw stays within the single merge (it does; addWireBundle pushes into the same geometries[] array).

designs.name: SYNAPSE BRIDGE — product-side faux-nerve forge
designs.oneLineConcept: The canon 3D brain stays untouched in its band; product-side SVG/canvas "synapse" tendrils grow OUT of the band's bottom edge, cross the layer boundary, and PLUG into the DOM editor / preview / command-line ports — replicating the wire shader's rainbow cable + flowing data packets + uBurst brighten + uHold dim, all driven by the SAME cognitionBus, so a real write travels a faux nerve and lights the surface it lands on.
designs.nerveWiring:
The work surfaces stay PLAIN DOM (Monaco/CodeCanvas, the LivePreview iframe, the CommandLine) exactly as now — no <Html>, no second canvas. The connection is a NEW product-only overlay component, SynapseBridge.jsx, rendered as a single fixed full-shell <svg> (or 2D <canvas>) sibling of the brain stage, pointer-events:none, z between the cosmos and the slabs. It is a REPLICA layer, never the real geometry (the real NervousSystem.tsx is canon + port-clobbered and is NOT touched).

Each faux nerve is a cubic-bezier path with TWO live anchors:
  • ROOT anchor (on the brain): the screen-projection of the canon wire-tip world point. SynapseBridge holds a ref to the live R3F camera (exposed product-side via a tiny accessor — either reading the existing canvas through a useThree bridge component mounted INSIDE the one canvas, or, to stay 100% outside the canvas, a fixed empirical root since the band is a fixed-FOV/fixed-camera framing). Project the three canon tips — left (-4.8,-1.7,0), right (+4.8,-1.5,0), spinal (0,-2.6,1.5) — PLUS the group sway (NervousSystem.tsx:166-168) and the Float bob, to the band's pixel space. Because the band is a fixed clamp(340px,52vh,560px) with the frozen tabX=4.82 framing, the roots land at stable band-relative percentages; the per-frame sway is added as a small sin/cos offset matching the exact group-drift formula so the faux root breathes WITH the real wire root (pitfall: must replicate groupRef sway + z=-1.2 or the tendril lags).
  • PORT anchor (on the DOM surface): a getBoundingClientRect() of the target surface's top-center — the editor pane top (.wb-editor), the preview pane top (.wb-preview), the command-line (.sb-command-line). A ResizeObserver + scroll/layout listener keeps these fresh.

The bezier sweeps from each band root, through the .sb-seam nebula (so the tendril literally emerges from the dissolving voyage), and terminates AT the port with a translate so the cable's end meets the panel's top edge — the same "plug into the top" contract the canon spinal bundle uses for the chat box. Three faux bundles map cleanly: LEFT band tip -> editor port, RIGHT band tip -> preview port, SPINAL tip -> command-line port. This restores the canon nerve->port topology across the layer boundary the broken shell severed.
designs.composition:
Vertical split-horizon, keeping the existing shell skeleton (shell.css band + cosmos + seam + dock) but RECONNECTED:

TOP BAND (clamp 340–560px): the ONE persistent <WorkspaceCanvas/>, the canon brain voyaging, untouched. The ported .topbar (sovereignty + Supervised pill + telemetry) and .approval-panel stay. The cramming side-consoles/terminal stay hidden by manufacturing.css (they don't fit) — BUT their nerves are no longer left dangling into dead ports, because the faux bridge re-homes the brain's reach to the real surfaces below.

SEAM: the nebula-bleed dissolve, now also the EMERGENCE zone — the three faux nerve bundles fan out of the band's bottom edge through this gradient, so the cables look like they grow out of the dissolving brain-space.

LOWER FORGE: three ports the faux nerves plug into —
  • EDITOR slab (left, flex 1.6): Monaco/CodeCanvas. Left faux nerve plugs its top-center.
  • PREVIEW slab (right, flex 1): LivePreview iframe. Right faux nerve plugs its top-center.
  • COMMAND LINE (dock bottom, full width): the directive input. Spinal faux nerve plugs its top.
The BuildFeed strip rides above the slabs as the narration readout (already bus-driven).

FUTURE TOOL-PORTS: a 4th/5th faux bundle is a one-line addition to the bundle array (root percentage + target ref), so a terminal, a test-runner, a file-tree each become a port the brain reaches with another tendril — no lab work, the whole point of the replica being data-driven.
designs.controlBehaviour:
The brain "controls a tool" by a DATA PACKET visibly travelling its faux nerve to that tool, then the tool lighting. SynapseBridge subscribes to cognitionBus and runs a requestAnimationFrame loop (one rAF for the whole overlay, paused when document.hidden):

  • CONTINUOUS PACKET FLOW (canon replica): each faux nerve carries a packet via the same math as the shader — packetT = fract(x*4 - t*speed + phase) with a smoothstep band — rendered as a bright dash/glow gliding along the bezier (SVG: an offset-distance dot on the path, or stroke-dashoffset animation; canvas: a gradient stamp at the arc-length point). Per-nerve speed/phase mirror the per-wire aSpeed(0.5–2.0)/aPhase attributes. Colors come from the canon COLORS[] palette (in-file constants) so the cable reads as the same rainbow braid.
  • uBURST (a write surges): on 'directive' the bundle to the command-line + all bundles flare; on 'agent-dispatch' "tool engaged: <tool>" the SPECIFIC nerve for that tool's surface surges — packet density up, stroke brightness ×(1+burst), a discrete bright packet SHOT fired from root to port that, on arrival, pulses the target port ring (the editor/preview/command-line gets a brief synapse-glow at its top edge). This is the literal reading: the write travels the nerve and lights the tool it lands on. Burst decays with the same damp-toward-0 curve.
  • uHOLD (approval quiet): on 'approval-required' every faux nerve dims to 0.3 and the packet flow slows/halts — mirroring mix(1.0,0.3,uHold) — so the whole organism (real wires up top + faux wires below) goes quiet together while the mind defers; restores on resolve/directive. Because both layers read the same bus, they quiet in the same frame.
  • BREATH: a gentle sin(t)·pulse glow modulation on the cables, phase-locked to the same clock, so faux cables breathe with the cortex.

Tool->surface routing table (product-side): write_file/create_file/edit_file/str_replace -> EDITOR nerve; a preview-affecting result (e.g. file saved / build) -> PREVIEW nerve; directive -> COMMAND-LINE nerve; verify/test -> can flash a dedicated future test-port nerve. Default unmapped tools surge the spinal/command nerve.
designs.themeFit: The mind is not frozen in its band looking down at dead tools — it REACHES. Its nervous system grows out of the voyaging brain, crosses into the forge, and grips each work surface; when it acts, you SEE the thought travel the nerve as a packet and arrive, lighting the tool. That is the sacred theme made literal at the manufacturing form: an autonomous superbrain travelling the infinite, now extending its own nerves to manufacture WITH its hands, never severed from them. uHold makes the supervised-mind thesis visible — the operator watches the whole organism hold its breath, wires and tendrils alike, while it defers for approval. The cosmos+seam keep the forge in the SAME deep space, so the brain hasn't left the voyage to come to an IDE; it brought the voyage to the workbench and wired into it.

designs.feasibility:
PRODUCT-SIDE (the whole bridge, NO lab edit, NO `npm run port`):
  • SynapseBridge.jsx (new product file) — the SVG/canvas faux-nerve overlay.
  • Subscribing to cognitionBus (subscribeCognition is an exported lib import) — packets/burst/hold sync.
  • getBoundingClientRect/ResizeObserver port anchoring to the DOM slabs — pure DOM.
  • Wiring real writes to light the right nerve: aiosAdapter ALREADY publishes 'agent-dispatch' detail "tool engaged: <tool>" for every tool_call (publishStep, aiosAdapter.ts:110-117) and 'knowledge-acquired' on tool_result — SynapseBridge just reads them. Making the EDITOR/PREVIEW actually reflect the agent's file writes (so the packet's arrival is truthful) is product wiring in Workbench.jsx (subscribe + apply file changes), not lab.
  • All CSS, the cosmos/seam reuse — product (shell.css is already product).
  • Un-hiding is NOT required; the faux bridge intentionally leaves the cramped consoles hidden and reaches the NEW surfaces instead.

LAB + `npm run port` (NOT needed for this direction, stated honestly):
  • Nothing here requires editing NervousSystem.tsx / SuperbrainHUD.tsx / SuperbrainScene.tsx. We do NOT re-route real endpoints, do NOT add a real nerve bundle, do NOT touch the shader. That is the explicit advantage of the faux-bridge path: zero canon risk, zero re-golden of the 3D geometry.

GPU REALITY (16GB laptop + local inference + Monaco + iframe):
  • The overlay adds ZERO WebGL cost — it's one SVG/2D-canvas layer, cheaper than the avoided <Html transform> reprojection. A handful of bezier strokes + a few packet dots at 60fps is trivial vs the cortex Voronoi (the single heaviest pass) and the 115-tube braid already running.
  • Mitigations baked in: single rAF for the whole overlay; pause on document.hidden and when not in manufacture mode; cap packet count; debounce port-rect reads to layout/resize not every frame; keep the LivePreview iframe debounced (it already remounts on srcDoc).

HONEST FIDELITY CEILING: this is a REPLICA, not the canon shader. It matches the 2D READ — same palette, same fract() packet flow, same burst-brighten/hold-dim behavior on the same bus — but it has NO depth occlusion, NO volumetric 115-tube braid pile, and its root tracks the brain via a projection/empirical approximation, not the true vertex transform. So it reads as "the brain's nerves faithfully reaching the tools," which is honest and on-theme, but it is NOT byte-identical to the real wires and MUST be validated in HIS browser (before/after screenshots, FIDELITY laws) before it ships. The canon brain + its 3 real nerves up top stay 100% untouched, so there is no regression risk to the sacred scene.
designs.functionalWiring:
The agent's REAL actions already flow on the bus; the bridge makes them visible and makes the surfaces truthful:

1. WRITE PATH (truth, then light): when a turn runs, aiosAdapter.publishStep emits {type:'agent-dispatch', detail:'tool engaged: write_file', label:'WRITE_FILE'} on tool_call and {type:'knowledge-acquired', label:'WRITE_FILE', detail:<output>} on tool_result (aiosAdapter.ts:110-161). SynapseBridge maps the tool name -> EDITOR nerve and fires a packet shot root->editor-port + burst; on tool_result it pulses the editor port ring (the write "landed"). To make the landing TRUE (not just lit), Workbench.jsx subscribes to the same bus / a shared turn store and applies the agent's file create/edit to its `files` state so CodeCanvas shows the new content and LivePreview re-renders — closing the loop the current Workbench explicitly defers ("wiring the agent's own writes ... is a later increment"). This needs the adapter to surface the file path+content of a write (extend the tool_result payload product-side, or read the backend's write events); the nerve+packet is the VISUAL, the file-state apply is the FUNCTIONAL.

2. DIRECTIVE PATH: CommandLine.submit already calls sendDirective (CommandLine.jsx:5,24), which publishes 'directive' BEFORE the turn — SynapseBridge surges the COMMAND-LINE nerve (packet up into the brain band, reading as the directive entering the mind) and the scene's existing burst fires. One pipeline, both layers react.

3. PREVIEW PATH: when files change (agent write or human edit), LivePreview re-renders; SynapseBridge fires a packet down the PREVIEW nerve so "the preview updated" reads as the brain pushing the result to the preview surface.

4. APPROVAL PATH: an 'approval-required' (human_required pause) dims every faux nerve + stalls flow (uHold), and the kept .approval-panel surfaces the diff; 'approval-resolved' restores flow. The forge visibly holds its breath with the brain.

Net: every real tool the agent runs becomes a port the brain commands down a (faux) nerve, and the editor/preview/command-line are no longer disconnected DOM — they are the brain's manufacturing hands, lit by the actual turn.
designs.risks:
• ROOT TRACKING DRIFT: the faux root must follow the canon wire tip across the brain's per-frame group sway (NervousSystem.tsx:166-168, z=-1.2) + Float bob. If it reads a fixed band-percentage without the sway offset, the tendril root lags the real wire and the seam looks detached. Mitigate by replicating the exact sway formula (or, cleaner, mounting a tiny useThree projector INSIDE the one canvas that writes the projected tip pixel to a shared ref each frame — still product-side, no 2nd canvas).
• FIDELITY VALIDATION GATE: it's a replica; it can read as "fake wires" if the palette/packet/glow don't match. Must do before/after screenshots in HIS browser per the FIDELITY laws before shipping; do not let it drift from the canon look.
• PORT-RECT THRASH: reading getBoundingClientRect every frame forces layout. Read only on resize/scroll/mode-change, cache between.
• rAF + bus contention: keep ONE rAF, pause when hidden / not in manufacture, cap packets — or the overlay competes with inference for main-thread time.
• TRUTHFUL LANDING: if the packet arrives but the editor doesn't actually show the write (because the file-state apply isn't wired), the bridge becomes theater. The visual (nerve) and functional (file apply in Workbench) must ship together, or the brain "controlling the tool" is a lie — the very failure the redesign exists to fix.
• NEVER a 2nd canvas: the optional in-canvas projector must be a child of the existing <Canvas>, not a new one (pitfall: re-runs boot, doubles GPU, cross-wires the module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM/SCENE_UNIFORMS singletons).
• If the seam emergence is misjudged, faux nerves can look like they float over the brain rather than grow from it — tune the bezier start to sit just inside the band's bottom edge, behind the seam gradient.

designs.name: FULL IN-SCENE CONTINUUM — the forge inside the voyage
designs.oneLineConcept: The entire manufacturing form is ONE continuous 3D scene: the canon brain voyages above, its three unchanged nerve bundles drop down and plug into the editor / preview / command surfaces — each a drei <Html> panel anchored at a real wire port — so a write literally rides a packet down a nerve and lights the tool it lands on; no DOM-band, no DOM-below, no second canvas.
designs.nerveWiring:
The crux is that nerve endpoints are HARDCODED 3D coords; faithful connection means the work surfaces must sit AT those coords, anchored the EXACT way the canon consoles are. Concretely:

PORT MAP (verified):
- LEFT nerve bundle terminates at group-local (-4.8, -1.7, 0.0) (NervousSystem.tsx:204, 292). Canon panel: SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1046 <Html position={[-4.8,-1.7,0.0]} zIndexRange={[100,0]}> with inner translate(-50%,-100%) (line 1047) — NO drei `transform` prop.
- RIGHT nerve bundle terminates at (4.8, -1.5, 0.0) (NervousSystem.tsx:205, 307). Canon panel: SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1097 <Html position={[4.8,-1.5,0.0]}> same translate(-50%,-100%).
- SPINAL bundle terminates at (0.0, -2.6, 1.5) (NervousSystem.tsx:321, juts +Z toward camera). This is a RENDEZVOUS not a geometric bind — the command bar is screen-space CSS in #hud-portal-root.

THE WIRING DECISION (honest): the three canon ports were sized for ~350px consoles bottom-anchored. An editor + preview need real estate AT new world points (e.g. editor port near (-3.4, -1.6, 0) widened, preview port near (3.4, -1.6, 0)). Two truthful routes:

ROUTE A (product-only, ports unchanged — the cheap-faithful path): Build a NEW product component `ForgePanels.tsx` mounted as a sibling of <SuperbrainHUD/> INSIDE the single existing <Canvas> (WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:230-243 tree). It mounts its OWN <Html position={[-4.8,-1.7,0]} zIndexRange={[100,0]}> wrapping the Monaco editor and <Html position={[4.8,-1.5,0]}> wrapping the LivePreview, copying byte-for-byte the canon translate(-50%,-100%) anchor and the no-`transform` projection-pin. The unchanged nerves project to those identical world points, so the REAL wire tips plug straight into the editor/preview — zero lab edit, zero shader change, because the file is NEW, not ported. The canon consoles are repurposed/hidden by a product CSS override (relocate their content INTO the forge panels' headers, never blanket display:none into dead ports). The command surface re-homes to where (0,-2.6,1.5) projects (CommandLine.jsx placed there) so the spinal bundle still meets it, and the .command-bar::after wire-glow gradient is reproduced product-side so the plug doesn't read dead.

ROUTE B (small lab+port, ports re-homed — the bolder full continuum): To give the editor/preview proper width and put the brain visually ABOVE the tools, re-aim the three bundles in the LAB (GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/components/canvas/NervousSystem.tsx): widen leftTargetX/rightTargetX, lower the port-entry Y so panels sit lower in frame, and optionally add a 4th addWireBundle (the helper already takes arbitrary control points, NervousSystem.tsx:207-263) for a future tool-port. Move the matching <Html> positions in SuperbrainHUD.tsx together (they are doubled hardcodes — move both or the wire dangles). Then `npm run port`, re-tag canon, re-golden, prove parity in HIS browser. This is the only way to get the bold "brain-above-tools" framing with full-width surfaces and a genuinely NEW port.

RECOMMENDED: ship ROUTE A first (proves the continuum 100% product-side, fidelity untouched), then graduate the chosen layout to ROUTE B as a budgeted canon change. NEITHER route touches the shader; both keep brain scene + nerve geometry canon.
designs.composition: A single vertical 3D column inside the one persistent <Canvas>, framed by the existing camera (position [0,0.25,8.5], 45deg FOV — WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:209). Top third: the canon brain + cortex + aura, voyaging, breathing, unchanged, still inside <Float>. Mid-frame: the three nerve bundles fan out of the brainstem (deepCore→stemExit→spinalDrop) and descend. Bottom two-thirds (where the nerves terminate): the work surfaces as <Html> panels — EDITOR plugged into the left port, PREVIEW plugged into the right port, COMMAND surface plugged into the spinal rendezvous at bottom-center. The cosmos behind is the REAL scene background (#010307 + fog, WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:227-228) and the canon sky/surface — NOT a cheap CSS cosmos div. The topbar (sovereignty row + Supervised pill + telemetry) stays as the canon screen-space #hud-portal-root overlay (free, trusted). The approval-panel stays too. There is exactly ONE continuous space: you read the brain commanding the forge below it through cables, because nothing is composited as a separate DOM band — the editor literally hangs off the left cable, the preview off the right, the command line off the spine.
designs.controlBehaviour:
The brain controls a tool by an event on the cognitionBus (lib/cognitionBus.ts) — the SAME synchronous module-singleton pub/sub the canon scene already subscribes to. Reading order of a live write:

1. A directive arrives: SuperbrainHUD.handleSubmit publishes {type:'directive', intensity:1} (SuperbrainHUD.tsx:808). SuperbrainScene sets burstRef.intensity>=0.6 (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1052). The module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM is mutated every frame from burst.current.intensity (NervousSystem.tsx:86,192-194), so finalColor *= (1+uBurst) (NervousSystem.tsx:73) — ALL wires surge bright. You see cognition leave the brain.

2. The agent runs a tool: aiosAdapter already publishes {type:'agent-dispatch', detail:'tool engaged: write_file'} (aiosAdapter.ts:111-114). The scene pushes burst 0.45 + a region thought-wave. The flowing DATA PACKET (flow = fract(vUv.x*4.0 - uTime*vSpeed + vPhase), packet smoothstep band, NervousSystem.tsx:63-64,71) rides down the bundle that feeds the touched surface — a write packet travels the LEFT cable into the editor, a build/render packet travels the RIGHT cable into the preview. The packet ARRIVING at the port is the moment the panel should flash its own ingress glow (product-side: subscribe the panel to the same event, pulse a CSS box-shadow keyed to the port color).

3. The mind defers for approval: 'approval-required' eases the shared SCENE_UNIFORMS.uHold 0→1 (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1024-1038); wires dim to 0.3 (mix(1.0,0.3,uHold), NervousSystem.tsx:75), the cortex amber dims, breath freezes — one signal, whole organism HOLDS. The forge panels read this same uHold (product-side subscriber) and desaturate/quiet too, so the editor visibly waits with the brain. On approval-resolved/directive/synthesis uHold eases back and the forge re-lights.

So "the brain controls a tool" is not a metaphor here: burst surge = the mind acting, packet flow = the command in transit down the specific nerve, hold dim = the mind waiting. Every present and future tool becomes a port simply by emitting on this bus — the wiring to surge/quiet it already exists; no shader edit to make a new port light up.
designs.themeFit: This is the boldest expression of "an autonomous AI-OS superbrain travelling constant into the deep-vast knowledgeable infinite space" because the forge is not a workshop the brain visits — it is part of the brain's own body, hanging off its nerves, voyaging WITH it. The cosmos behind the editor is the same infinite the brain sails; the editor and preview drift in that same void, tethered to the mind by living cables that carry its thoughts as light. The mind is never frozen: even while you type, the brain breathes above, packets flow down the cables, the aura voyages. You are not editing in a tool beside the AI — you are editing inside the AI's nervous system, watching it command your files down its own spine. The nervous system reads as exactly what the operator clarified: the control bus, the way the mind reaches out and moves its whole world.

designs.feasibility:
PRODUCT-SIDE (no lab edit, no `npm run port`) — ships ROUTE A entirely:
- NEW file `ForgePanels.tsx` mounted as a sibling of <SuperbrainHUD/> in the existing <Canvas> tree (WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:230-243). It owns <Html> wrappers at the existing -4.8/+4.8 ports wrapping CodeCanvas (Monaco) + LivePreview, copying the canon no-transform + translate(-50%,-100%) anchor. New file = product-editable, not clobbered.
- Repurpose (NOT blanket-hide) the canon consoles via product CSS override; re-home CommandLine.jsx to the spinal projection; reproduce the .command-bar::after wire-glow product-side.
- Light the surfaces: aiosAdapter ALREADY publishes 'tool engaged: <name>' (aiosAdapter.ts:111-114); the forge panels subscribe to cognitionBus (exported lib, importable product-side) for ingress-glow + uHold quiet.
- All editable per constraints: main.jsx, SuperbrainApp/Shell.jsx, new product files + product CSS overrides.

GENUINELY NEEDS LAB + `npm run port` (ROUTE B only, optional/bolder):
- Re-aiming leftTargetX/rightTargetX/spinal control points or ADDING a 4th addWireBundle in NervousSystem.tsx (port-clobbered manifest file) — for full-width surfaces + brain-above framing + a new tool port.
- Moving the <Html> anchor positions in SuperbrainHUD.tsx (also ported) — must move WITH the endpoints (doubled hardcode).
- These touch canon geometry → trigger FIDELITY laws: his-browser parity, before/after screenshots, canon re-tag + re-golden.

GPU (the honest cost — constraint 5): this direction is the HEAVIEST of the candidates because Monaco + a live iframe now composite over WebGL while the brain renders, and drei <Html> RE-PROJECTS its wrapper every frame as the brain sways. Mitigations that keep it viable on 16GB also running local inference: (1) use the no-`transform` projection-pin (canon does this) NOT drei transform — far cheaper, skips R3F matrix scaling; (2) keep Monaco in a STATIC-positioned inner wrapper so only the lightweight outer <Html> anchor reprojects, not Monaco's layout; (3) debounce LivePreview — it remounts on every keystroke (key={srcDoc}, LivePreview.jsx:117); park/freeze the iframe when the editor is focused or panel offscreen; (4) respect TIER_DPR and leave headroom — the cortex Voronoi is already the single heaviest shader (SuperbrainScene.tsx:733) and PostFX renders a fullscreen pass each frame.

HONEST BOTTOM LINE: the FAITHFUL continuum (real nerves plugging into real Monaco/preview) is achievable 100% product-side via ROUTE A at the existing ports. The BOLD full continuum (brain-above-tools, wide surfaces, a new nerve port) requires a modest, clean LAB+port canon change (ROUTE B) — feasible, but it IS a canon edit needing re-golden + his-browser parity. The GPU bill is real and is the main reason to start at ROUTE A and measure before committing to embedding full-width Monaco+iframe in the live scene.
designs.functionalWiring:
The agent's REAL actions already speak the bus, so the forge does real work, not theatre:
- WRITE PATH: aiosAdapter.ts:111-114 publishes {type:'agent-dispatch', detail:'tool engaged: <tool>'} on every real tool dispatch. ForgePanels subscribes (subscribeCognition) and, when detail starts 'tool engaged: write_file'/'edit_file', updates the editor's `files` state with the agent-written content and flashes the LEFT port ingress-glow. The same files object feeds LivePreview, so the agent's write flows brain→left-nerve→editor→right-nerve→preview as one continuous reaction.
- READ-BACK for the editor content: the adapter's tool events carry a `detail` label today; to surface the actual written file body the cleanest product path is a backend read endpoint (or the adapter passing the file payload in event.data — cognitionBus already supports a structured `data` field, cognitionBus.ts:45). ForgePanels reads event.data.path/content and sets editor state. No lab edit — both the adapter emission and the bus `data` field are product-side lib.
- DIRECTIVE PATH: the existing command surface (re-homed CommandLine.jsx) calls sendDirective; SuperbrainHUD already publishes {type:'directive'} before onDirective (SuperbrainHUD.tsx:808), which surges the nerves — so issuing a build from the spinal command surface visibly fires cognition down the spine, exactly the operator's "brain commands a nerve".
- APPROVAL PATH: a human_required pause publishes 'approval-required' → uHold rises → the canon approval-panel surfaces its diff (kept) AND the forge panels quiet; resolving it re-lights both. The supervised-mind thesis renders end-to-end through the forge.
- FUTURE TOOLS: any new tool (terminal, test runner, graph) becomes a port by (a) emitting 'tool engaged: <name>' (auto-surges its nerve, no shader edit) and (b) ROUTE A: a new <Html> at an existing port, or ROUTE B: a new addWireBundle aimed at the new surface. The bus is the universal control plane.
designs.risks:
- GPU is the dominant risk: full-width Monaco + a live iframe embedded as in-scene <Html> reprojecting every frame, atop the 115-tube bundle + cortex Voronoi + PostFX, on a 16GB laptop also running local inference. If it drops below the FIDELITY DPR tier it threatens the no-auto-degrade law. Mitigate with no-transform pins, static Monaco wrapper, debounced/parked preview; MEASURE in his browser before ROUTE B.
- Port-coincidence fragility: the nerve tips project to fixed pixels tuned for Z~7.5 / 45deg FOV / 16:9 (tabX=4.82 frozen, NervousSystem.tsx:171-174). If the camera reframes for "brain-above-tools" the tips land elsewhere and the plug separates — this is precisely the bug the broken shell hit by shrinking the band but leaving the projection. ROUTE A must keep the canon camera; ROUTE B must re-home endpoints AND verify projection.
- Doubled hardcode: leftTargetX/rightTargetX in NervousSystem.tsx and the <Html> positions in SuperbrainHUD.tsx are two separate ported literals that MUST agree — move both together in the lab or the wire dangles into empty space.
- Single-canvas invariant: ForgePanels MUST mount inside the existing <Canvas>; a second canvas re-runs BootSequence, doubles GPU, and cross-wires the module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM/SCENE_UNIFORMS singletons (safe only because the scene mounts once).
- Never blanket display:none a port (manufacturing.css:21-29 is the documented severing) — relocate/re-provide the panel at the same world point or the nerve renders into a dead port and reads lifeless.
- Burst vs hold source mismatch: new nerves/surfaces must mutate burst.current.intensity for surge but read SCENE_UNIFORMS.uHold for quiet (different sources, NervousSystem.tsx:86 vs 183) — mixing them means the surface won't surge on a write or won't quiet on a hold.
- ROUTE B is a canon change: re-tag + re-golden + before/after screenshots + his-browser parity are mandatory (FIDELITY laws) before any visual nerve re-route ships.
- Interaction cost: embedding the editor in-scene means pointer/scroll/focus events route through drei <Html> z-stacking (zIndexRange) over WebGL — verify Monaco keyboard capture and iframe focus don't fight the canvas pointer handlers.

designs.name: TOOL-PORT BUS — the brain commanding a rack of labelled tool-ports through its nerves
designs.oneLineConcept: The manufacturing form is a PORT RACK: the canon brain hangs in the docked band and every tool — editor, preview, terminal, and any FUTURE tool — is a labelled PORT the brain plugs a nerve into; a real agent action travels that nerve as a data packet and lights the port it lands on, and adding a new tool is literally adding one more port + one more nerve to the bus.
designs.nerveWiring:
The wiring is built on the truth that there are exactly THREE canon nerve endpoints in NervousSystem.tsx and they are hardcoded to a frozen camera: left bundle terminates at world (-4.8,-1.7,0), right at (4.8,-1.5,0), spinal at (0,-2.6,1.5), with the whole nerve group swaying each frame (NervousSystem.tsx:166-168) and tabX=4.82 frozen for Z=7.5/45deg/16:9 (NervousSystem.tsx:171-174). So I do NOT invent free-floating ports — I treat those three real endpoints as the THREE PRIMARY BUS SOCKETS and mount a NEW product in-scene panel at each one, reusing the EXACT canon anchoring of the side consoles.

PORT 1 (LEFT SOCKET, -4.8,-1.7,0) = EDITOR PORT. A new product file PortRack.jsx renders <Html position={[-4.8,-1.7,0.0]} zIndexRange={[100,0]}> (NO drei `transform` prop — projection-pinned, exactly like SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1046) whose inner div uses transform:'translate(-50%,-100%)' so the panel BOTTOM-CENTER lands on the wire tip. Inside it: a labelled port header ("PORT 01 · EDITOR · write_file") plus the Monaco surface (CodeCanvas). The wing-width of the brain band makes -4.8 the left third of the strip, which is exactly where an editor wants to sit.

PORT 2 (RIGHT SOCKET, 4.8,-1.5,0) = PREVIEW PORT. Same pattern, <Html position={[4.8,-1.5,0.0]}> + translate(-50%,-100%), header "PORT 02 · PREVIEW · render", wrapping the LivePreview iframe.

PORT 3 (SPINAL SOCKET, projects under bottom-center) = COMMAND / TERMINAL PORT. This nerve was NEVER a 3D-projected bind — it is a visual rendezvous against the 2D-CSS command bar (superbrain.css:1212, .command-bar::after wire-glow at :1239). So Port 3 stays a screen-space DOM port: the product CommandLine + a slim terminal feed placed where (0,-2.6,1.5) projects (bottom-center), and I REPRODUCE the .command-bar::after wire-glow gradient on it (a product CSS class .port--spinal::after) so the spinal nerve still visibly pours light into it. This keeps the command port faithful without any lab edit.

THE BUS ABSTRACTION (this is the assigned direction): each port is a declarative descriptor { id, label, tool, socket: 'left'|'right'|'spinal'|future, busEvent } rendered by PortRack from a PORTS array. The nerve→port binding is the socket field. The three canon sockets are "occupied"; a future tool is registered by pushing a descriptor. If its socket is one of the three canon sockets, it plugs into a REAL nerve with ZERO lab work (it just re-tenants that <Html> point). If it needs a brand-NEW socket coordinate (a 4th physical nerve), that is the one honest lab edit (option b below): one more addWireBundle() call in the lab NervousSystem.tsx aimed at the new port's world-point, + npm run port. The bus is the seam that makes "add a future tool" a one-line descriptor push for the common case and a single bounded lab edit for the rare new-socket case.

CRITICAL NON-NEGOTIABLES baked into the wiring: (1) PortRack mounts INSIDE the single existing <Canvas> as a sibling of <SuperbrainHUD/> in WorkspaceCanvas's tree — never a 2nd canvas (constraint 3). (2) I do NOT blanket-hide the consoles like manufacturing.css does; instead the editor/preview port panels REPLACE the consoles AT THE SAME world-points so the nerves never dangle into dead ports. (3) The panels are projection-pinned (no `transform`) to respect GPU + the canon pixel-perfect sizing trick (SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1040-1045).
designs.composition:
A single vertical voyage, top-to-bottom, all in the ONE infinite space:

TOP BAND (full width, ~46-52vh): the canon brain DOCKED smaller but unchanged — same scene, same cortex, same three nerve bundles streaming down out of the brainstem. The ported .topbar (sovereignty row FIDELITY·SKY·SURFACE + Supervised pill + live telemetry) stays pinned at the very top (kept, never hidden — it wires for free).

PORT RACK (the lower two-thirds of the band, where the nerves terminate): three labelled ports arranged as the nerves naturally fan — EDITOR PORT bottom-left (under the left bundle at -4.8), PREVIEW PORT bottom-right (under the right bundle at +4.8), COMMAND/TERMINAL PORT bottom-center (under the spinal bundle). The nerves visibly braid down from the brainstem and each plug-in glows where it meets its port header. The composition reads instantly as "brain on top, a rack of tools below it, nerves connecting them."

PORT LEDGER (thin strip, far left or along the bottom edge): a vertical list of all registered ports — "01 EDITOR ●  02 PREVIEW ●  03 COMMAND ●  + ADD PORT" — the visible manifest of the bus. Future tools appear here as new rows. This is the extensibility made legible: you SEE the rack has slots.

Below the band the existing cheap CSS cosmos + seam continue the infinite downward, so nothing reads as a hard UI floor — the rack floats in the same space the brain voyages through. The Voyage toggle returns to full-screen home. Everything is one persistent canvas; switching to manufacturing only resizes the stage and mounts PortRack.
designs.controlBehaviour:
The control read is: the brain ACTS on a tool → a data packet runs down that tool's nerve → the port lights. This is already 90% wired by the canon shader and the cognitionBus; the design only has to feed it the real signals.

CONTINUOUS (canon, untouched): every nerve already carries flowing data packets via the fragment shader fract(vUv.x*4.0 - uTime*vSpeed + vPhase) (NervousSystem.tsx:63-64) and breathes with uBreath. So at rest the rack already looks ALIVE — packets idle down every nerve into every port, the bus is "powered."

BURST (the brain commanding a specific tool): when an agent acts on a tool, the product write path publishes publishCognition({type:'agent-dispatch', detail:'tool engaged: write_file'}). SuperbrainScene already maps agent-dispatch → burst.current.intensity≥0.45 (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1066), which feeds the module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM (NervousSystem.tsx:86,193) and the shader brightens ALL wires via finalColor*=(1.0+uBurst) (NervousSystem.tsx:73). So a write SURGES the nerves — the packet visibly races and the cables flare. The PORT PANEL ITSELF also subscribes to the same bus and, on an event whose detail names ITS tool, runs a CSS flare on the port header (a 600ms glow + a packet-arrival pulse at the plug seam) so the surge reads as "this packet arrived at THIS port." That panel-side accent is the per-port targeting the global uBurst can't express (uBurst is one scalar for all wires).

HOLD (the mind defers): on approval-required, SCENE_UNIFORMS.uHold eases 0→1 (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1024) and the shader dims wires to mix(1.0,0.3,uHold) (NervousSystem.tsx:75) — the whole rack goes QUIET, packets dim, and the editor port shows the diff awaiting approval. On approval-resolved/directive it surges back. So the operator literally watches the nervous system hold its breath at a gate and resume on approval — the product's supervised-mind thesis rendered on the bus.

Net read: idle = powered bus, packets idling to every port; a real action = a burst racing the nerve + a flare on the exact port; an approval = the whole bus holds. No animation timers faked — every state is a real cognition event.
designs.themeFit: The core theme is an autonomous mind voyaging into infinite knowledge-space, and the operator's overlooked insight is that the nervous system IS the control bus — how the mind commands the whole screen. This design makes that literal and EXTENSIBLE: the brain is not parked at a workbench, it hangs in the same infinite space (cosmos continues below the band) still travelling, and it reaches DOWN its own nerves to operate a rack of tools. Controlling a tool is not a click — it is the mind sending a packet down a nerve into a port. The rack framing says the mind's reach is open-ended: there is always another socket, another nerve, another tool the voyaging brain can extend a tendril to. "Each and every present or future tool" becomes the design's spine — the Port Ledger literally shows empty slots and "+ ADD PORT," so the form embodies a mind whose nervous system GROWS to command more of its world as it voyages. The brain stays sovereign (it acts; the tools light up in response), never a passive editor with a brain decoration bolted on.

designs.feasibility:
PRODUCT-SIDE, NO LAB EDIT (the whole core ships here):
- PortRack.jsx — NEW product file mounted as a sibling of <SuperbrainHUD/> inside the ONE existing <Canvas>, rendering three <Html position={...}> port panels at the EXISTING canon endpoints (-4.8,-1.7 / 4.8,-1.5 / spinal-projected), reusing the canon non-transform + translate(-50%,-100%) anchor. Nerves plug in UNCHANGED.
- Hosting Monaco (CodeCanvas) and the LivePreview iframe inside those <Html> wrappers — drei + @monaco-editor/react are already product deps.
- The command/terminal port as screen-space DOM with a reproduced .port--spinal::after wire-glow — product CSS.
- Port flares + ledger + the bus subscription — product (publishCognition/subscribeCognition are exported lib).
- Replacing manufacturing.css's blanket display:none with RE-TENANTING the same world-points (the key fix that un-severs the nerves) — product CSS + PortRack.
- Emitting real agent actions as 'agent-dispatch tool engaged: <name>' — product write path / aiosAdapter already does this.

GENUINELY NEEDS LAB + npm run port (ONLY for new physical sockets, NOT for the core):
- A 4th/5th REAL nerve bundle for a future tool at a brand-new coordinate = one addWireBundle() call in lab GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/.../NervousSystem.tsx + the matching <Html> point — a canon change: re-tag, re-golden, his-browser parity (FIDELITY laws). This is bounded and clean (addWireBundle takes arbitrary control points) but it IS a canon edit. The honest framing: future tools that re-tenant one of the 3 canon sockets are pure product; future tools that demand their OWN nerve are a small lab job each.

GPU REALITY (16GB laptop + local inference): Monaco + an iframe through projection-pinned <Html> (NOT drei `transform`, which re-projects with matrix scaling every frame) is acceptable but not free, stacked on the 115-tube bundle + the cortex Voronoi (the single heaviest shader, SuperbrainScene.tsx:732) + the PostFX fullscreen pass. Mitigations: projection-pinned mode only; keep Monaco in a static-positioned inner wrapper so it doesn't reflow on sway; debounce LivePreview's key={srcDoc} remount (LivePreview.jsx:117); park/pause the preview iframe when offscreen; honor the FIDELITY DPR tier. Honest verdict: two heavy DOM surfaces in-scene is the real cost ceiling — if it stutters in his browser, demote the preview port to a projection-pinned DOM panel that merely SITS at +4.8 (still plugged, lighter).

BOTTOM LINE: the full faithful form — real nerves plugging into real editor/preview/command ports, lighting on real agent actions — ships 100% PRODUCT-SIDE. Lab is reserved strictly for growing the rack a NEW physical nerve.
designs.functionalWiring:
The forge must DO the work, not mime it, so the bus is wired to the agent's real action stream end-to-end:

1. WRITE PATH → EDITOR PORT. When the agent emits create_file/edit_file, the product write handler (the same place aiosAdapter already publishes "tool engaged: <name>") does two things: (a) updates the editor's file model so Monaco shows the real new/changed content, and (b) publishCognition({type:'agent-dispatch', detail:'tool engaged: edit_file', label:<path>}). The bus event drives uBurst (the nerve surges) AND the EDITOR port panel — which subscribed to the bus — runs its arrival flare because the detail names its tool. So a real file write literally travels the left nerve and lights the editor.

2. PREVIEW PATH → PREVIEW PORT. On a write that affects the rendered doc, the preview port rebuilds srcDoc (debounced) and publishes 'agent-dispatch tool engaged: render'; the right nerve surges, the preview port flares, the iframe updates. The preview shows the REAL output, not a placeholder.

3. DIRECTIVE PATH → COMMAND PORT. The product CommandLine reuses sendDirective; on submit it publishes {type:'directive'} (as SuperbrainHUD.handleSubmit does at :808) BEFORE dispatching, so the spinal nerve surges and the terminal logs the real turn. Agent turn events (republished by the adapter) append to the terminal feed in the command port.

4. APPROVAL PATH → WHOLE-BUS HOLD. When a turn hits human_required, the adapter publishes approval-required → uHold eases up → all nerves dim → the editor port surfaces the pending diff and the .approval-panel (kept) shows it. Operator approve/reject publishes approval-resolved → bus resumes. This is real supervised execution, visible on the nerves.

5. ADD-A-PORT (the extensibility payoff, functional). Registering a future tool = pushing a descriptor to the PORTS array { id, label, tool:'run_tests', socket:'spinal'|'left'|… , busEvent:'agent-dispatch' }. PortRack renders it at the socket's world-point; its panel subscribes to the bus filtered on its tool name. The moment the agent actually invokes run_tests and publishes 'tool engaged: run_tests', the new port flares on the real event — no scene edit, no shader edit, just the descriptor (if it re-tenants a canon socket) or that descriptor + one lab nerve (if it needs a new socket). The wiring is generic over tools, which is the whole point of the bus.

Every light on the rack is downstream of a real agent action on the cognitionBus — nothing is timer-faked except the canon idle packet flow.
designs.risks:
1. CAMERA-FRAMING DRIFT (highest): the nerve tips are frozen to Z=7.5/45deg/16:9 (tabX=4.82). Docking the brain into a SHORTER band changes the effective framing, so the tips project to different screen pixels and the port panels separate from the plugs — this is exactly what made the shell's nerves dangle. MITIGATION: keep the docked stage at the canon aspect (letterbox the band, don't squash it) so projection is preserved, and validate the plug alignment in HIS browser before anything else. If the band must be shorter than canon allows, the plug truth requires a lab re-route (option b) — say so honestly rather than hiding panels.

2. GPU CEILING: Monaco + iframe + 115 tubes + Voronoi + PostFX on a 16GB inference machine may stutter. MITIGATION as in feasibility (projection-pinned, static Monaco wrapper, debounced/parked preview, DPR tier). Honest fallback: demote preview to a lighter DOM panel at the port.

3. PORT-CLOBBER TEMPTATION: it will be tempting to "just nudge" leftTargetX or a console <Html> position to fit the editor — that is a ported file, overwritten by npm run port, and a canon change. MITIGATION: never edit superbrain/ product-side; move the PANEL (product) not the NERVE; reserve NervousSystem.tsx edits for genuine new sockets in the lab.

4. uBURST IS GLOBAL, NOT PER-PORT: the shader can't brighten only one nerve, so cross-port "which tool fired" is carried by the panel-side flare, not the wires. Acceptable, but be honest that the wire surge is whole-bus; per-port targeting is a CSS accent. A truly per-nerve glow would need a new shader uniform = lab edit.

5. SPINAL PORT IS A RENDEZVOUS, NOT A BIND: treating the command port as 3D-anchored is the documented mistake; it must stay screen-space DOM with the reproduced ::after wire-glow, or the brain-to-bar connection reads dead.

6. SECOND-CANVAS FOOTGUN: any future-tool work that spawns its own scene re-runs boot + doubles GPU and cross-wires the module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM/SCENE_UNIFORMS singletons. Ports MUST dock into the one canvas.

ground.controlSignals:
THE TRANSPORT IS THE cognitionBus (frontend/src/superbrain/lib/cognitionBus.ts) — a module-singleton synchronous pub/sub (subscribeCognition/publishCognition, lines 52-67), NOT props. Both the 3D scene AND the DOM HUD subscribe to the same event stream, which is precisely why they read as one organism. Event types (cognitionBus.ts:13-32): knowledge-acquired, directive, burst, agent-dispatch, synthesis, approval-required, approval-resolved, telemetry. EVERY tool, present or future, becomes a port by emitting on this bus; the brain 'commands a nerve' by the scene reacting to that event.

THE NERVE SHADER UNIFORMS (NervousSystem.tsx WIRE_FRAGMENT, lines 38-83): three live channels carried along the wires —
1. DATA PACKETS: uTime drives a flowing packet per wire — flow = fract(vUv.x*4.0 - uTime*vSpeed + vPhase); packet = smoothstep band (NervousSystem.tsx:63-64,71). Per-wire aSpeed/aPhase/aPulse attributes (NervousSystem.tsx:243-245,256-259) make each cable carry its own high-speed pulse. uTime is the shared SCENE_UNIFORMS.uTime leaf (SuperbrainScene.tsx:184, written once/frame at line 1166).
2. uBurst (COGNITION BURST): brightens the wires — finalColor *= (1.0 + uBurst) (NervousSystem.tsx:73). Driven NOT from the shared uniform but from a module-level WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM (NervousSystem.tsx:86) mutated every frame from burst.current.intensity (NervousSystem.tsx:192-194). That burst ref is owned by SuperbrainScene: a 'directive' event sets intensity>=0.6 (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1052), 'agent-dispatch' tool-engaged sets >=0.45 (1066), SKILL MASTERED sets =1 (1088), and an autonomous periodic burst fires every ~8s + republishes a 'burst' event (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1152-1159). Decays via damp toward 0 (1160). So a real write travelling the wire == a literal burst surge on the nerve.
3. uHold (APPROVAL QUIET): the nervous system goes quiet while the mind defers — finalColor *= mix(1.0,0.3,uHold) (NervousSystem.tsx:75). uHold IS the shared SCENE_UNIFORMS.uHold leaf (SuperbrainScene.tsx:175 passes uniforms.uHold into the material, NervousSystem.tsx:183). It eases 0->1 on approval-required and back on resolve/directive/synthesis (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1024-1038), damped in the frame loop (1143-1148). The SAME uHold dims the cortex amber and freezes breath — one signal, whole-organism.

THE BREATH: uBreath (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1170-1175) is a shared systole/swell/tide rhythm written once/frame; the wires breathe via the local sin(uTime...) term modulated by per-wire aPulse (NervousSystem.tsx:67,72), phase-locked to uTime so the cables breathe with the cortex.

A plain-DOM editor outside the canvas cannot have a 3D nerve enter it. Feasible bridges, honest tradeoffs:

CON: GPU — Monaco + an iframe rendered through drei <Html> (which re-projects every frame) on a 16GB laptop also running local inference is the real cost (constraint 5). Mitigate by NOT using drei `transform` (use the cheaper projection-pinned mode like the originals) and by making the editor a normal scrollable DOM panel that merely SITS at the port, not a transformed-in-3D surface. uBurst on the wires comes from a MODULE-LEVEL WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM (NervousSystem.tsx:86,193), NOT the shared SCENE_UNIFORMS, while uHold/uTime DO come from the shared leaf.
