The UNCHANGED nerves project to those same world points, so they plug straight in with ZERO lab edit and ZERO shader change. This is product-editable because the new file is new, not a ported file. Caveat: the canon panels are only ~350px wide and anchored bottom-up; a full editor needs more real estate, so you likely WIDEN/re-home the world points — which is the boundary into option (2). You can move the PANEL freely product-side; you cannot move the NERVE without a lab edit. If the panel's anchor drifts from the hardcoded -4.8/+4.8, the plug visibly separates.

(2) RE-ROUTE NERVE ENDPOINTS (LAB + PORT) — the only way to make nerves meet panels at NEW positions. leftTargetX/rightTargetX/the spinal (0,-2.6,1.5) are hardcoded in NervousSystem.tsx (a port-manifest file). Moving a port to e.g. the editor's top-center, or adding a 4th bundle for a new tool, REQUIRES editing GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/components/canvas/NervousSystem.tsx + npm run port. Feasible and clean (the addWireBundle helper takes arbitrary control points), but it's a canon change: re-tag + re-golden, prove parity in his browser.

(3) PRODUCT-SIDE FAUX-NERVE OVERLAY (SVG/canvas tendrils) bridging the 3D brain band to DOM panels BELOW the canvas — needed only if work surfaces live OUTSIDE the canvas (the broken shell's layout). Doable product-only: a fixed SVG/canvas layer drawing bezier tendrils from the band's bottom edge into DOM panel tops, animated with a JS replica of the packet flow (fract(x - t*speed)) and subscribed to cognitionBus for burst brightening/hold dimming. Honest fidelity ceiling: it can mimic the 2D look (color palette COLORS[] is in-file, the packet math is portable) but NOT the real thing — no depth occlusion, no shared shader, the braid's volumetric pile (115 merged tubes, dynamic spiral radius) is impractical to reproduce in SVG, and it won't track the brain's per-frame 3D sway without reading the same projection. Reads as 'good imitation wires', acceptable as a stopgap, never byte-canon. Cannot touch the real shader (locked).

(4) HYBRID (RECOMMENDED): keep work surfaces as in-scene <Html> at re-homed ports — option (1) for layout + a SMALL targeted option (2) to aim the nerves at the new anchors — so the real nerves, real shader, real packets plug into the real Monaco/preview. Avoids the faux overlay's fidelity tax entirely.
ground.productVsLab:
PRODUCT-SIDE, NO LAB EDIT (the cheap wins):
- A NEW in-scene panel component using <Html position={...} zIndexRange> at the EXISTING hardcoded port world-points (-4.8,-1.7 / 4.8,-1.5), reusing the canon translate(-50%,-100%) anchor — nerves plug in unchanged. New file, not a ported file.
- Re-homing the COMMAND BAR port: it was never a 3D-projected panel (it's CSS left:50%/bottom:34px in #hud-portal-root). A product CSS override + a product command line (CommandLine.jsx already exists and reuses sendDirective) can occupy that role. The spinal nerve's screen landing is fixed, so to keep the plug you place the new bar where (0,-2.6,1.5) projects.
- Lighting ANY new tool's nerve: publishCognition on the existing bus ('directive'/'agent-dispatch tool engaged: X') — already drives uBurst + thought-waves. No shader, no lab.
- Hiding/showing/restyling consoles, adding product CSS overrides of ported classes, the cosmos/seam/dock wrappers — all product (this is what shell.css/manufacturing.css already do).
- Mounting Monaco/iframe INSIDE an <Html> wrapper — product (drei + monaco are product deps).

GENUINELY NEEDS LAB EDIT (GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/...) + npm run port:
- MOVING or ADDING nerve endpoints (NervousSystem.tsx leftTargetX/rightTargetX/spinal points, tabX, or a new addWireBundle call for a 4th port). Hardcoded in a manifest file; product copy is overwritten by port.
- Any change to the wire SHADER (packet speed, new uniform like a per-port 'active tool' glow, hold/burst behavior) — NervousSystem.tsx WIRE_FRAGMENT.
- Changing the shared uniform contract or how the scene root maps bus events to burst/wave/hold (SuperbrainScene.tsx) — ported.
- Changing the consoles' own <Html> anchor positions inside SuperbrainHUD.tsx — ported (so prefer a NEW product panel over editing the ported one).
HONEST BOTTOM LINE: a faithful redesign where surfaces sit AT the canon ports is ~product-only; a redesign that relocates the ports to fit a bigger editor/preview/new tools is a lab+port job — modest and clean, but it IS a canon change needing re-tag + re-golden + his-browser parity.
pitfalls: NEVER mount a 2nd <WorkspaceCanvas/> / <Canvas> for the work surfaces — re-runs BootSequence and doubles GPU (booted never resets; WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:255). All in-scene panels must live in the ONE existing canvas tree.

The consoles are NON-transform <Html> (flat panels pinned to a projected point), NOT <Html transform> (true 3D-CSS planes). Do not 'fix' them to transform: it changes sizing/AA, re-enables R3F matrix scaling the canon deliberately skips (SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1040-1045), and breaks the pixel-perfect plug. Replicate the non-transform + translate(-50%,-100%) pattern.

The nerve endpoints are HARDCODED to a specific camera (Z~7.5, 45deg FOV, 16:9) — tabX=4.82 is frozen ON PURPOSE so the geometry doesn't rebuild 60x/s (NervousSystem.tsx:171-174). Docking the brain into a SHORTER band (the shell's --sb-band-h ~52vh) changes the effective framing; the nerve tips then project to different screen pixels and the plug separates. The broken shell hid the panels but left this mismatch — that's why nerves dangled.

Monaco mounts its OWN editor surface, web workers, and DOM; inside an <Html> it composites over WebGL as a normal DOM layer (fine) but every brain sway re-projects the <Html> wrapper each frame — keep Monaco in a STATIC-positioned wrapper, don't let dr8 reflow it. The 16GB machine also runs local inference; Monaco + a live iframe + the 115-tube bundle + cortex Voronoi (the single heaviest shader, SuperbrainScene.tsx:733) compete for GPU. Respect the FIDELITY tier (TIER_DPR) and consider pausing/parking the preview iframe when not visible.

The iframe LivePreview re-creates its document on every keystroke (key={srcDoc} remounts, LivePreview.jsx:117) — acceptable in the flat DOM, but if embedded as <Html> the remount + reproject churn compounds. Debounce or it stutters the voyage.

Hiding the panels the nerves plug into (manufacturing.css display:none on .left-console/.right-console/.command-bar) SEVERS the bus's visible targets but leaves the nerves rendering into empty space — the exact lifeless failure. If you hide a port, you must RE-HOME its nerve (lab) or RE-PROVIDE a panel at the same world-point, never just delete it.

uBurst is a MODULE-LEVEL singleton (WIRE_BURST_UNIFORM) and SCENE_UNIFORMS is module-scope — safe ONLY because the scene mounts exactly once. A 2nd canvas or a 2nd NervousSystem instance would cross-wire/double-write these. One instance, always.

FIDELITY IS SACRED + PORT-CLOBBER: any edit inside frontend/src/superbrain/ (consoles, shader, scene) is overwritten by npm run port and is really a LAB change requiring canon re-tag + goldens + parity in HIS browser. Prefer NEW product files and product CSS overrides; reserve lab edits for genuine endpoint/shader re-routing and budget the re-golden.

The command-bar 'plug' relies on .command-bar::after, a hand-placed wire-glow gradient (superbrain.css:1239) faking light from the wires; if you replace the command bar, you lose that cue unless you reproduce it — the brain-to-bar connection reads dead without it.
